speaker: interviewer
text: November 16, 1995, tape number five. Survivor, Nechama Shneorson. Interviewer, Charlotte Rettinger. In Flushing, New York. So why don't you pick up where we left off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since she was married, and we found out that her husband is alive, and is in Kovno, and the Russians didn't let anybody out of there anymore. They closed up the-- the-- the-- the doors for coming in, yes, but not out. She decided to go to him. And separated again, and she went back to Kovno. Since I was only left alone, and I had no parents, they put me in-- they called it kibbutz for children. Actually, it was a-- called it a kibbutz, but it was really for orphans, children that had no parents, in Lodz. I'm talking still we are in Lodz. It's called a kibbutz. We were all youngsters there without parents, tried ourselves to make ourselves as happy as possible. Some schooling, we had some teachers. But actually, what we were there together is to help us to realize that our future is not going to be back in Kovno and Lithuania, but we have to all of us try to go to Israel. There's no other future but Israel. OK, we were all very happy about it. We'll have a new home again. And it will be all wonderful if we just get it into a train or a boat and-- and get there. But it wasn't meant for us because the Russians were all over at this point in the eastern of Europe. And they were very unhappy to let the people go. So we were supposed to hide, and travel nights, and cross borders at night that nobody should see us, and go from place to place like that. And sometimes grab a train somewhere, so we can have a little ride on the train. And we wind-- we came to Czechoslovakia. We stayed there for a while in a camp. And from Czechoslovakia, we carried on, continued until we got to Germany. In Germany, since the Americans, they liberated so many of the concentration camps, were more Jews left. So they opened up DP camps, Displaced Persons camps. And so the people could relax a little bit and choose their ways where they want to go, and what they want to do with the continuation of their lives. I was brought into a place called Landsberg am Lech, which is about 60 miles away from Munich, again, in a kibbutz because there was no other place where to keep me. And until a very dear friend of my family from Kovno found out that I was there. There was a father and a mother and a son that they all three of them survived. Lieberman is their names. And they decided that since I'm all alone, they will adopt me, and they will try to help me the best they can.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they brought me to Munich. This is where they lived. They asked me to go to school, which I did. I went back to music school, the Conservatorium of Munich, a music school, which was one of the best in-- in those-- before the war, music for the voice to train, and learn all the other things that belongs to acting for the opera. I was going for the opera, and I was very happy with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And meanwhile, I was also studying my regular studies, which I wanted to finish high school. I didn't have it during all these years, that five years that I lost of the war. So I tried to do that, and also, do a little acting, actually acting, which I got into a group of actors. And we used to do that in Landsberg and travel around to other Displaced Persons camps. It was a lot of fun. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until my sister came, actually, with her husband. They managed to escape, which is a story that she will have to tell herself, a story that could take people apart, how they managed to escape. But they were there, and they took me away from that family, because she said, you don't have to be adopted by anybody if I'm here. And we went up to be in Landsberg am Lech again, together. It's one of the most happiest moments to be together, my only sister. And we stayed there for three years because actually, nobody knew what the future is going to bring and not knowing yet where we should wind up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her husband, which is have his father, and mother, and my girlfriend were all together. And we were all together in Landsberg. So we had a family back together. And we, as children, my girlfriend, myself, waited for them to make up their minds where we're gonna go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since they couldn't, meanwhile, my sister became pregnant. So one day, for some reason, I said, how long are we going to wait? I was by that time, I was already 18 years old. Israel had the Independence War going on. And I somehow made a decision by myself. I'm going to go there and try to help whatever we can. And since my sister is pregnant, let her wait until she'll have the baby. And I will be in Israel and prepare something that she should come to me. She should have a home to come to with the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: My girlfriend came along with me. She left her parents there, and her brother, and my sister. And the two of us, on our own, we went to the Jewish Agency, where we registered. And we came to Israel. We were put-- they took us. It was, again, a new running, a new escaping from border to border until we got to France. In France, we found-- they found for us a little boat, a little fishing boat. Only 400 people could get into it, all the way in the bottom of the boat. And we took off to Israel. It took us 10 days. We got to Israel. No water, no food, but we were used to it already. So it wasn't such a big thing. At least we knew we are going for a new future.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came. We got in direct into the Army because our registering was direct to go to the Army. It was not to go there to a private life. 18 years old, you have to do Army. We came to the Army. We went through all what-- what you have to do in the Army. And we both wind up to be in the Air Force. They chose her for parachute [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] putting together. What do you call it? The parachute-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had to put together the right part. The parachute has to be put together correctly. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there's-- there's a name for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the radar. And we were, for two years, in the Army until I met my husband. Actually, I met my husband much earlier than that. But when we decided to get married, we-- I was allowed to leave the Army. Once you get married, you didn't have to be in the Army. So the struggle started a private life, to get into a private life, which I haven't known yet how.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what it means to live normal--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I was 11 years old. So that was a new concept for me, a new life, a new-- a new unknown thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were get married-- we were married in 1950. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The times were terrible in Israel in those days. There was nothing there. And since we didn't have much, we didn't need much. And life was going on struggling. My sister, at this point, made a decision. She will not come to Israel. She will go with her husband to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a tragedy for me because we were very poor in Israel. We really had nothing. I couldn't offer anything to my husband because I came with empty hands. My husband was, since he was nine years old, in the Army, trying to help to prepare, like every other young man in those days. They there-- they-- they were all only for their country and not having much of education because everything was only the country to prepare for throwing the-- the-- the English out, and-- and-- and fighting for life for themselves. And so there was no much education to become a professional. And it was a struggle. To see my sister was just a dream to me, which I couldn't even dream of it. And it took me 13 years the first time that I put together some money to come to visit her here. And how it was then, in a very cute way, I went to work for a travel agency, so I can get a free ticket to travel because otherwise, I wouldn't have been able to. Which I did, and the first year after I was done the first year in the travel agent, they gave me a ticket, and I came to visit my sister. And here, I find a sister that we haven't seen each other for 13 years. I'm a married woman already with a child. She has three. At this point, no, she had only two at this point. It was 1960. She only had two daughters, two grown up people with different lives, with different backgrounds. Background, the same background, but very different backgrounds after the war. The United States was not Israel, and Israel was not the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first couple of hours, I had the problem to communicate. I really didn't know who is she. I think she felt the same way. But at 4 o'clock in the morning, after we talked and tried to give whatever we could in words, it hit the moment we looked at each other deeply in our eyes, and we fell apart. And we were, you know, we are really sisters, aren't we?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then everything fell off again. It was nothing anymore. There's no need to talk. We-- we-- we know how we feel. It's not important who we became. It's what we are now, and that's it, and that's the most important thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with her for three months. I just couldn't leave her. Being with them was so important to me. We-- we were communicating, don't misunderstand. We were in letters constantly from those 13 years. We knew of each other everything, but of course, correspondence. But how can you explain that by sitting together, and looking at each other, and holding hands, and just looking and saying, how can I ever leave that woman again? I'll never be able to. And I came back home, and I asked my husband, would he mind to make a new life and to try, to try, so I can be a little bit more with my sister? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very happy in Israel. As little that we had, as is little life put together that we could, I was a very happy person there. But to be with my sister was the most happiest. Right, and your husband agreed?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, and he, and your daughter came here when?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1964. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we are here until-- since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What has your life been like since you've been here? Do you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a new struggle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: None of us knew English that well to immediately to start out a normal life. I actually knew maybe, maybe 100 words in English. My husband knew more because he studied in Israel always English. I didn't have a chance to study. We went to school in the evenings. We worked during the days. Start to work in Alexander's in the show department. He was driving a truck, an oil truck for five years in Long Island, and bringing oil to the people's homes. It was a very hard job, very hard. He then found somebody that took him into a nursing home, and he became-- he studied, and he became a food service manager. And he became a-- a professional. And he did that in a couple of nursing homes until he found a hospital that they needed a man like that. And he moved into a hospital, and he would stay there for 25 years until he retired. My life was a little bit more shaky. And I moved on from place to place, like from Alexander's to Bloomingdale's. When I felt a little stronger, I went to a private company selling curtains. From the curtains, I went into a company that was making fabrics. And I stayed in textile business for also over 25 years, not in one company. I moved on from one company to another. Actually, three companies through this. Didn't move around that much. I was in one company a year, and they went broke down. So I went into another. It was 12 years in one and 13 years in the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you retired now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm-- this is my second year that I am retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell me a little about your daughter. What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's name is Edie, because-- spell it E-D-I-T-H, but she likes to be called Edie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is actually after my sister Ida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it should just ring a little bit better. In Hebrew, her name is Idit. Idit means the best of the best soil that brings out the best qualities. And that's her name, and it sounds a little bit like Edith. So that's why we gave her the name, and she is the best of all. She's a beautiful human being. She had a lot of trouble to-- first year, to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] acclimatize herself in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very unhappy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was very unhappy. But since she did, she Americanized herself very, very well, and very fast after that. And she finished school. She finished to be a computer programmer. Never worked at it. She didn't like it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she became, also, the garment center, became very active and worked herself up into a vice presidency in a very wonderful company. They closed up, so she moved on to turn the clock around in a very different way. And she became a business manager for one of the largest car dealers, Potamkin. And that's where she is now, Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she lives on her own. She's a beautiful person, beautiful human being. And we have a lot of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], what they say, from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: A good friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to add as a final statement to the interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like that very much. I don't have that paper. That's left in there. I'll have to remember it by heart. I want to dedicate this taping to my daughter, Edith Shneorson, and to all the future generations that will come. They should know and never forget what Hitler did to me, to my family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to six million Jews. That's all I have to ask, and I'll be the happiest if they will. Thank you very, very much for listening to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have on the left side is my mother. Standing up near her, her sister, two brothers. Sitting, grandmother and grandfather, and a little brother in the back, in this end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose-- whose brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's. My mother's family,

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's-- that's her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have some idea of the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kovno, Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother when she was 16, which would make it about in 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really have no idea where it was done. I'm sorry. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father at their wedding day, not in white or in a tuxedo because they both-- they eloped. And they ran away from my-- my mother's parents because my grandparents didn't want my father to be part of her life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my left, my mother. On the right, my father. Sitting down, Grandma, Grandpa, keeping me on his lap. My older sister in the middle, and the younger sister on the side. Was in the backyard of my grandparents' house in Kovno, Lithuania. One of the most beautiful days I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my full family in 1932, full family portrait. My mother all the way in the middle in the top, father right next to her on the right, my older sister on her left. Grandma holding on her lap my little sister, Genia. In the middle is Ida, and myself sitting right on my father's lap, a child of three. Beautiful memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my four-- we are the four sisters here together. I would say this is in 1939. My big sister [? Laurie, ?] which is called now is Zlata. On my right, Ida, on the left, Genia in the middle. Myself, right next on the right of my older sister, just a year before the war broke out, happy moments. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say this is the last one taken before the war broke out, 1940 in Lithuania, anyway, mother and father. We found a picture in a-- under a bed, broken in pieces. That's all we could take out is these two faces to make something we should have to-- have something to remember them by. Beautiful two people, young and beautiful, and they're not with us anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell us the story about this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] This is a picture of my graduation on 1940, '39, '40. Fourth class that we were done with. And it was a very big party after that made with high dignitaries that they all came to our school. And we had a very big show. And at this point, I was supposed to sing my very first, debuted on stage all by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think could--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am the fifth from the left side, sitting right in front of the flowers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sing that song for us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try if I will be able to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I do it now?

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be great.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so don't film my face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Unless--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This song I'm going to sing will be the same song I sang then on stage, a Hebrew song by name 'Maybe.' [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. (SINGING IN HEBREW)

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 'Maybe.' Did I see the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which is in Israel, or didn't I? Yes, I did. Thank you so much. Thank you. It was beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't believe I did it. Oh, my daughter is gonna plotz. [LAUGHS]
